internal.content:
import HomepageTemplate from './Template.js'; export default HomepageTemplate; # PREFIX ## Chosen From Static Site Generators I personally lack a deep understanding of front-end technology stacks. Front-end frameworks evolve fast without reasons. For instance, you and your classmates might have initially been interested in jQuery, and you probably know Bootstrap, a CSS framework that can create colorful webpages using jQuery under the hood. However, in just a few years, we have React options like Ant Design or Material-UI. This means you need to adapt to new technology stacks, or you might even find yourself unfamiliar with terms like ‘MVVM’. What’s crucial to understand is that in front-end development, what you may label as an ‘error’ isn’t always an error, what you call an ‘exception’ isn’t always an exception, and even the concept of data types can be ambiguous. Web pages can sometimes contain multiple errors in the browser’s console log and run pretty good. However, the situation changes when it comes to your script code, which can throw tons of dependency errors, even if you’ve configured everything cautiously. Learning front-end development can be challenging, and building a personal website from scratch may seem unrealistic. Many people believe that building a blog should only contain writing Markdown, which can be directly converted into HTML or CSS files, regardless of the underlying tech stack, such as the use of a Go-based site generator like Hugo. Static site generators create static pages with just a few configurations, often involving editing JSON or YAML files. Users can focus on content creation in Markdown. However, that results in less customizable. The experience of using them tell me one thing: “They are not modifiable”. Often, we use Hugo templates from Hugo theme showcases or other GitHub repositories, and we are constrained by the SCSS they provide. I rarely can make any modifications towards typography/theme. Setting aside those previous comments, our primary focus is on UI/UX, with typography as a close second, Markdown support is also considered essential. ### Customization Issues With IBM Carbon & Gatsby I still did lots of customization even I did use this static site generator (won’t go into too many details here). IBM Carbon offers users a React option. However, it seems that the vanilla configurations don’t include Markdown support, which is a pity. Accidentally, I found that there exists a non-vanilla support version based on Gatsby, and here’s why I chose this framework to build a site. #### It Takes Forever To Compile Its disadvantages are apparent. Gatsby’s compilation speed is slow, and the files in the .cache directory don’t simultaneously update to reflect changes by: ```bash npm run dev:clean ``` Still, the actual coding process remains closely tied to React’s component-based, also, it is the strength of this framework. #### Still Not Support Katex As you may see, this article updated at 10/22/2023 when IBM Carbon or IBM Carbon Gatsby teams both have not built in Katex yet.

Futher Details Will Be Discussed In Next Article.

import HomepageTemplate from './Template.js'; export default HomepageTemplate; # PREFIX ## Chosen From Static Site Generators I personally lack a deep understanding of front-end technology stacks. Front-end frameworks evolve fast without reasons. For instance, you and your classmates might have initially been interested in jQuery, and you probably know Bootstrap, a CSS framework that can create colorful webpages using jQuery under the hood. However, in just a few years, we have React options like Ant Design or Material-UI. This means you need to adapt to new technology stacks, or you might even find yourself unfamiliar with terms like ‘MVVM’. What’s crucial to understand is that in front-end development, what you may label as an ‘error’ isn’t always an error, what you call an ‘exception’ isn’t always an exception, and even the concept of data types can be ambiguous. Web pages can sometimes contain multiple errors in the browser’s console log and run pretty good. However, the situation changes when it comes to your script code, which can throw tons of dependency errors, even if you’ve configured everything cautiously. Learning front-end development can be challenging, and building a personal website from scratch may seem unrealistic. Many people believe that building a blog should only contain writing Markdown, which can be directly converted into HTML or CSS files, regardless of the underlying tech stack, such as the use of a Go-based site generator like Hugo. Static site generators create static pages with just a few configurations, often involving editing JSON or YAML files. Users can focus on content creation in Markdown. However, that results in less customizable. The experience of using them tell me one thing: “They are not modifiable”. Often, we use Hugo templates from Hugo theme showcases or other GitHub repositories, and we are constrained by the SCSS they provide. I rarely can make any modifications towards typography/theme. Setting aside those previous comments, our primary focus is on UI/UX, with typography as a close second, Markdown support is also considered essential. ### Customization Issues With IBM Carbon & Gatsby I still did lots of customization even I did use this static site generator (won’t go into too many details here). IBM Carbon offers users a React option. However, it seems that the vanilla configurations don’t include Markdown support, which is a pity. Accidentally, I found that there exists a non-vanilla support version based on Gatsby, and here’s why I chose this framework to build a site. #### It Takes Forever To Compile Its disadvantages are apparent. Gatsby’s compilation speed is slow, and the files in the .cache directory don’t simultaneously update to reflect changes by: ```bash npm run dev:clean ``` Still, the actual coding process remains closely tied to React’s component-based, also, it is the strength of this framework. #### Still Not Support Katex As you may see, this article updated at 10/22/2023 when IBM Carbon or IBM Carbon Gatsby teams both have not built in Katex yet.
